More Notes: The study explored how climate suitability for dengue transmission changed due to climate and population factors, highlighting a significant increase in global populations living in high-risk areas.
The global population living in high-risk areas increased from 48.8% to 60.0%.
Proportion of land area with high climate suitability increased from 38.4% to 39.5%.
Population in high-risk settings increased from 59.6% to 64.2%.
Population in high-risk settings increased from 63.1% to 72.3%.
Population in high-risk settings increased from 25.6% to 34.9%.
Population in high-risk settings increased from 38.6% to 42.1%.
Independent impact of climate change: 2.16 billion more people in high-risk areas.
Independent impact of population growth: 182 million more people in high-risk areas.
High and very high density areas globally showed an increase in climate suitability for DENV transmission from 58.5% to 66.2%.
Very high density areas globally showed a significant increase in climate suitability for DENV transmission from 48.1% to 63.6%.
Upper-middle income economies saw an increase in land area with high climate suitability from 31.6% to 32.9%.
High income economies experienced an increase in land area with high climate suitability from 20.5% to 22.2%.
Significant increase in climate suitability in temperate zones, from 25.2% to 30.5%.
Tropical climate zones experienced an increase in high climate suitability from 87.5% to 90.1%.

Approximately 70% of the populations in Africa and Asia are currently living in areas with a high risk of infection, a roughly 15% increase relative to four decades earlier, assuming favorable conditions for vector establishment.
